serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried beansprouts in Singapore is approximately 150g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion served alongside rice or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Beansprouts — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Beansprouts (cr_id F14080033). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — bean sprouts and low-carbohydrate cooked vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried beansprouts with soy sauce seasoning. GI of 25 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked mung bean sprouts and comparable low-carbohydrate cooked vegetables (GI range 15–32). Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as: (25 × 2.59 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.97, rounded to 1.0. This is a very low glycemic load consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 411mg per 100g — exceeds the 400mg per 100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100g and request preparation with reduced soy sauce and no added salt to bring sodium intake below 330mg per serving; low-sodium soy sauce is a recommended substitute.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 2.59g per 100g with 2.42g dietary fibre and minimal sugar (1.57g per 100g), producing a negligible glycemic load of approximately 1.0 per 150g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried beansprouts are an excellent low-glycemic vegetable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio minimises postprandial glucose excursion, though sodium content should be monitored if hypertension is a comorbidity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium at 411mg per 100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; fat content is moderate at 3.45g per 100g from cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, this dish should be prepared with minimal oil and low-sodium soy sauce to reduce sodium below 300mg per 100g; the high dietary fibre (2.42g per 100g) and plant-based composition are otherwise cardioprotective.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 411mg per 100g exceeds the 400mg RA threshold, potentially exacerbating RA-related tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce and no added salt; beansprouts themselves are anti-inflammatory and beneficial, but sodium from seasoning is the primary concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried beansprouts (炒豆芽, Tauge goreng) is a common Singaporean and Chinese vegetable side dish made by wok-frying fresh mung bean sprouts with aromatics such as garlic and ginger, seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and salt. The dish is cooked over high heat for a short time, preserving the characteristic crisp-tender texture and mild, slightly nutty flavour of the beansprouts. It is a staple accompaniment to rice and noodle dishes across Singapore, Malaysia, and southern China. Nutritionally, stir-fried beansprouts are low in calories (55 kcal per 100g) and carbohydrates (2.59g per 100g), with moderate protein (2.58g) and fat (3.45g) from cooking oil. Dietary fibre is notable at 2.42g per 100g. The primary clinical concern is sodium at 411mg per 100g, which marginally exceeds the 400mg threshold relevant to hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load (GI approximately 25, GL approximately 1.0 per 150g serving). It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly. The dish contains soy and is not gluten-free due to soy sauce. Preparation variations include the addition of dried shrimp, fish cake slices, or chilli, which would alter the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080033) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match to the named dish in the primary regional approved source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked mung bean sprouts, which reduces GI confidence to medium for that specific field; overall record confidence remains high.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 0.5g per 100g based on typical vegetable oil composition used in stir-frying; HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific stir-fried beansprouts preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14080033 as of 2026-04-16. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked mung bean sprouts; no direct GI measurement for this specific seasoned preparation exists in the approved GI source. Keto dietary tag from input retained with caution: at 2.59g carbohydrate per 100g the dish is compatible with ketogenic dietary patterns, but the soy sauce seasoning should be verified for added sugars in keto-strict contexts.
